title: Graphs
description: Graph problems test your ability to model relationships and traverse them efficiently. Master adjacency list representation with Map, DFS/BFS traversal, and connected component detection.

sections.title: Adjacency list with Map
sections.explanation: Represent graphs as a Map where each key is a node and its value is an array of neighbors. Use `map.get(node) || []` for safe neighbor access. For undirected graphs, add edges in both directions. This is the standard interview representation.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph with edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-3. Print the neighbors of node 0 and node 3, each as comma-joined strings.

validation.message: Node 0 connects to 1,2. Node 3 connects to 1,2 (sorted).
Use Map for adjacency list and push for adding neighbors

exercise.hint: For undirected edges, push u to v's list AND v to u's list.

sections.title: DFS on a graph
sections.explanation: Graph DFS uses a visited Set to avoid cycles. Start from a node, mark visited, then recurse on unvisited neighbors. This traverses all reachable nodes. DFS can be recursive or iterative with an explicit stack. Use it for path finding, cycle detection, and topological sort.

exercise.prompt: Implement DFS on graph: 0->[1,2], 1->[3], 2->[3], 3->[]. Collect visited nodes in order from start node 0. Print them joined by commas.

validation.message: DFS from 0: visit 0, then 1, then 3 (via 1), then 2
Use a Set for visited tracking with add and has

exercise.hint: visited.add(node) marks it, visited.has(neighbor) checks before recursing.

sections.title: BFS on a graph
sections.explanation: Graph BFS uses a queue and visited Set. It processes nodes level by level, making it optimal for shortest-path in unweighted graphs. Enqueue the start, then for each dequeued node, enqueue unvisited neighbors. BFS guarantees the first time you reach a node is via the shortest path.

exercise.prompt: BFS on graph: 0->[1,2], 1->[0,3], 2->[0,4], 3->[1], 4->[2]. Start from 0. Collect nodes in visit order and print joined by commas.

validation.message: BFS from 0 visits level by level: 0, then 1,2, then 3,4
Use shift for queue dequeue and Set for visit tracking

exercise.hint: shift() dequeues from front, visited.add(nb) marks the neighbor.

sections.title: Connected components
sections.explanation: To find connected components, iterate all nodes and run DFS/BFS from each unvisited node. Each traversal discovers one component. Count the number of traversals for the component count. This pattern appears in island counting, friend groups, and network connectivity problems.

exercise.prompt: Count connected components in a graph with 6 nodes and edges: 0-1, 1-2, 3-4. Nodes 5 is isolated. Print the component count.

validation.message: Check visited.has() and increment components for each new DFS
